message.content: can you use devops-mcp server installed in this environment to diagnose why synology-monitor MCP server in this Claude for Windows environment keeps failing?
can you use devops-mcp server installed in this environment to diagnose why synology-monitor MCP server in this Claude for Windows environment keeps failing?

content.text: Let me start by checking the devops-mcp server health and then look for information about the synology-monitor MCP.

input.command: find / -name "*synology*" -o -name "*synology-monitor*" 2>/dev/null | grep -v "Permission denied" | head -50

content.text: Good, I can see the synology-monitor project. Let me dig deeper into both locations.

input.command: find /worksp/monitor/app/packages -name "*.json" | xargs grep -l "mcp\|server\|claude" 2>/dev/null | head -20
find /worksp/monitor/app -name "*.json" | xargs grep -l "mcp\|claude_desktop\|claude-desktop" 2>/dev/null | head -20
find /worksp/monitor/app/apps -maxdepth 2 -type d 2>/dev/null

content.text: Found the MCP server — it's at `/worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-mcp`. Let me dig into it.
Good, now I have a clear picture of the code. Let me check if the service is running, look for the config file it needs, and check environment variables.

input.command: find /worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-mcp -name "tools-config.json" 2>/dev/null; find /worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-mcp/dist -name "tools-config.json" 2>/dev/null

structuredContent.stdout: /worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json

input.command: docker ps -a 2>/dev/null | grep -i "synology\|nas-mcp\|mcp" || echo "No docker"; ps aux | grep -i "nas-mcp\|synology\|node.*dist" | grep -v grep

261	    "enable_scheduled_task": "WRITE: Enables a disabled DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI — pass task ID from check_scheduled_tasks — always asks your approval first",
262	    "disable_scheduled_task": "WRITE: Disables a DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI — suppresses runaway/broken tasks without deleting them — always asks your approval first"
263	  }
264	}"}

156	  "_tool_descriptions": {
157	    "check_system_info": "DSM version, NAS model, uptime, CPU, memory — baseline context for any conversation",
158	    "list_volumes": "Discovers active NAS data volumes and their filesystem usage so later tools do not assume everything lives on /volume1",
159	    "list_shared_folders": "Lists DSM shared folders with their real paths and selected metadata",
160	    "inspect_mounts": "Shows the live mount graph for volumes, package storage, and bind mounts",
161	    "inspect_encryption_state": "Checks whether volumes and share paths appear mounted, unlocked, and visible to packages",
162	    "check_disk_space": "Shows disk and inode usage for all active data volumes",
163	    "check_hardware_temps": "CPU/chassis temperature sensors, fan speeds, and SMART disk temperatures",
164	    "check_volume_health": "DSM-layer RAID/volume health (synovolumestatus, synoarraystatus), mdstat, SMART overall",
165	    "check_cpu_iowait": "Measures how much time the CPU is stuck waiting on disk — high values mean an I/O problem",
166	    "check_agent_container": "Confirms the Synology Monitor agent container is running",
167	    "get_resource_snapshot": "Full picture: top processes, disk activity, network connections, memory, and recent errors — best starting point for any problem",
168	    "check_io_stalls": "Finds processes stuck waiting on disk, measures disk queue depth, and looks for hung task warnings in the kernel",
169	    "check_memory_detail": "Full meminfo, swap activity rates, dirty/writeback pages, OOM kill history",
170	    "check_network_health": "Interface errors/drops, routing table, DNS resolution, listening ports, NFS/SMB connections",
171	    "check_tailscale": "Tailscale VPN status — interface state, IP, and whether the daemon is reachable",
172	    "check_network_connections": "Active TCP connections per process, connection state counts, top peers, Drive sync connections",
173	    "check_security_log": "Failed logins, security events, admin audit entries, SSH connections",
174	    "check_active_sessions": "Currently active SMB, NFS, SSH, DSM web, and Synology Drive sessions",
175	    "check_packages": "All installed DSM packages, their running status, and recent package events",
176	    "tail_system_log": "Recent /var/log/messages — general kernel and service events",
177	    "tail_drive_server_log": "Shows the most recent Synology Drive server log entries",
178	    "search_drive_server_log": "Searches Drive server logs for a specific word, share name, or error",
179	    "tail_sharesync_log": "Shows the most recent ShareSync log entries across all volumes — what files are being synced and any errors",
180	    "check_sharesync_status": "Looks specifically for stuck, conflicted, or erroring ShareSync tasks across all volumes",
181	    "check_kernel_io_errors": "Reads the kernel error log for disk errors, SCSI faults, and filesystem corruption",
182	    "check_share_database": "Lists all shared folders from the DSM database — failures indicate database corruption",
183	    "check_drive_package_health": "Checks whether the Synology Drive package is properly installed across all volumes",
184	    "check_drive_database": "Checks Synology Drive's internal databases for corruption across all volumes",
185	    "search_webapi_log": "Searches DSM WebAPI logs for share access errors and authentication failures",
186	    "search_all_logs": "Searches every log file on the NAS for a specific word or phrase",
187	    "find_problematic_files": "Finds files with names that break ShareSync — special characters, conflict files, and extremely long names",
188	    "check_filesystem_health": "Checks filesystem mount status, inode usage, RAID status, and SMART disk health across all volumes",
189	    "check_scheduled_tasks": "Lists all DSM scheduled tasks and their last run result — catches silent cron failures",
190	    "check_backup_status": "Checks Hyper Backup status and recent backup log entries across all volumes",
191	    "check_container_io": "Shows which Docker containers are doing the most disk I/O",
192	    "check_package_runtime": "PHASE-1B: Checks a specific package's runtime state — synopkg status, enabled flag, PID files, lock files, matching processes",
193	    "check_daemon_processes": "PHASE-1B: Shows process state for key Synology daemons: synologand, invoked, syncd, cloud-control, syno_drive_server",
194	    "inspect_package_lockfiles": "PHASE-1B: Finds stale lock files across all packages and runtime dirs that may prevent package starts",
195	    "inspect_crash_signals": "PHASE-1B: Looks for crash evidence — segfaults, OOM kills, core dumps, DSM error log events",
196	    "tail_package_logs": "PHASE-1B: Shows recent log entries for a named DSM package from all known log locations",
197	    "search_package_logs": "PHASE-1B: Searches all log files for a named DSM package and a filter term",
198	    "inspect_path_metadata": "PHASE-1E: Shows POSIX metadata for an exact path — owner, group, mode, size, inode, timestamps",
199	    "inspect_path_acl": "PHASE-1E: Shows POSIX and Synology ACL entries for an exact path via getfacl and synoacltool",
200	    "inspect_effective_permissions": "PHASE-1E: Shows effective access on a path with optional per-user group membership and share-level access check",
201	    "check_smart_detail": "PHASE-1C: Full SMART attributes, error log, and self-test history for a disk (all disks or a specific device)",
202	    "check_scrub_status": "PHASE-1C: Btrfs scrub status for all volumes and RAID sync/check progress from mdstat",
203	    "check_storage_pool_detail": "PHASE-1C: Detailed storage pool and RAID array state from DSM and mdadm — finds degraded arrays and rebuilding disks",
204	    "check_btrfs_detail": "PHASE-1C: Btrfs filesystem usage, device error counters, balance status, and subvolume list for all Btrfs volumes",
205	    "check_disk_error_trends": "PHASE-1C: Compact SMART error table for all disks — reallocated sectors, pending sectors, uncorrectable errors, temperature",
206	    "check_volume_quota_and_inode_pressure": "PHASE-1C: Inode usage pressure and Btrfs qgroup quota state for all data volumes",
207	    "check_interface_flaps": "PHASE-1D: Interface carrier change counts, error counters, and link state — detects unstable physical connections",
208	    "check_bond_health": "PHASE-1D: Bonding/LACP state, slave health, and bond mode if bonded interfaces are configured",
209	    "check_dns_and_gateway_health": "PHASE-1D: DNS resolution tests, default gateway ping, and nameserver/route configuration",
210	    "check_service_ports": "PHASE-1D: Listener state and connection count for SMB, NFS, SSH, DSM, Drive sync, LDAP, and rsync ports",
211	    "check_synology_drive_network": "PHASE-1D: Drive sync port 6690 listener state, connection counts by client, and recent Drive network errors",
212	    "find_recent_path_changes": "PHASE-1E: Files modified within lookback_hours under an exact path, sorted by mtime",
213	    "find_path_versions_and_snapshots": "PHASE-1E: Btrfs snapshots, recycle bin entries, and Drive version hints for an exact path",
214	    "search_file_access_audit": "PHASE-1E: Searches DSM file access audit logs for a path fragment or username",
215	    "search_smb_path_activity": "PHASE-1E: Searches Samba/SMB logs for activity related to a path, share, or username",
216	    "search_drive_path_activity": "PHASE-1E: Searches Drive server and ShareSync logs for activity related to a path or username",
217	    "hash_file": "PHASE-1E: SHA-256 and MD5 hashes plus timestamps for an exact file path — verify integrity or detect corruption",
218	    "compare_file_versions": "PHASE-1E: Compares two file paths by metadata, hashes, and optional text diff",
219	    "collect_incident_bundle": "PHASE-1F: Targeted diagnostic snapshot for an incident type: drive, storage, network, permission, or crash",
220	    "fetch_log_file": "PHASE-1F: Returns the content of a specific log file path — lists available logs if no path given",
221	    "fetch_package_db": "PHASE-1F: Queries a named package's SQLite database — lists tables or runs a specific SQL query",
222	    "fetch_support_artifacts": "PHASE-1F: Lists available DSM support bundles, large log files, package log dirs, and core dumps",
223	    "run_command": "Run any read-only command the AI needs for deep diagnosis — blocked automatically if it tries to write anything",
224	    "restart_monitor_agent": "WRITE: Restarts the Synology Monitor agent container — always asks your approval first",
225	    "stop_monitor_agent": "WRITE: Stops the Synology Monitor agent — always asks your approval first",
226	    "start_monitor_agent": "WRITE: Starts the Synology Monitor agent — always asks your approval first",
227	    "pull_monitor_agent": "WRITE: Downloads the latest monitor agent version — always asks your approval first",
228	    "build_monitor_agent": "WRITE: Rebuilds the monitor agent container — always asks your approval first",
229	    "restart_nas_api": "WRITE: Restarts the NAS API container — always asks your approval first",
230	    "restart_synology_drive_server": "WRITE: Restarts the Synology Drive package — always asks your approval first",
231	    "restart_synology_drive_sharesync": "WRITE: Restarts the ShareSync package — always asks your approval first",
232	    "restart_hyper_backup": "WRITE: Restarts the Hyper Backup package — always asks your approval first",
233	    "rename_file_to_old": "WRITE: Renames a problem file by adding .old — always asks your approval first",
234	    "remove_invalid_chars": "WRITE: Removes sync-breaking characters from a filename — always asks your approval first",
235	    "trigger_sharesync_resync": "WRITE: Forces a ShareSync re-sync by restarting ShareSync — always asks your approval first",
236	    "restart_synologand": "WRITE: Restarts the synologand daemon via synoservice — always asks your approval first",
237	    "restart_invoked_related_services": "WRITE: Restarts the invoked daemon and related DSM scheduler services — always asks your approval first",
238	    "restart_scheduler_services": "WRITE: Restarts crond/DSM scheduler services — always asks your approval first",
239	    "restart_network_service_safe": "WRITE: Restarts a named network service (smb, nfs, ssh, etc.) via synopkg or synoservice — always asks your approval first",
240	    "start_btrfs_scrub": "WRITE: Starts a Btrfs scrub on one or all data volumes — always asks your approval first",
241	    "start_smart_test": "WRITE: Starts a SMART short or long self-test on a specific disk — always asks your approval first",
242	    "create_prechange_snapshot": "WRITE: Creates a read-only Btrfs snapshot as a recovery point before changes — always asks your approval first",
243	    "list_snapshot_candidates": "PHASE-3: Lists Btrfs snapshots under all volumes with their path, creation time, and size — find restore candidates",
244	    "list_drive_version_history": "PHASE-3: Shows Drive-managed version history for a file path — lists available versions and their timestamps",
245	    "inspect_recycle_bin": "PHASE-3: Lists recent files in share recycle bins (#recycle) across all volumes — find deleted files for recovery",
246	    "check_smart_test_progress": "PHASE-4: Shows in-progress SMART self-test completion percentage and ETA for all or a specific disk",
247	    "generate_support_bundle": "WRITE: Generates a DSM support bundle and saves it to /tmp — always asks your approval first",
248	    "cancel_smart_test": "WRITE: Cancels an in-progress SMART self-test on a specific disk — always asks your approval first",
249	    "cancel_btrfs_scrub": "WRITE: Cancels an in-progress Btrfs scrub on a volume — always asks your approval first",
250	    "set_vm_overcommit_memory": "WRITE: Sets vm.overcommit_memory live via sysctl — always asks your approval first",
251	    "persist_vm_overcommit_memory": "WRITE: Persists vm.overcommit_memory to sysctl.conf for reboot survival — always asks your approval first",
252	    "clear_package_lockfiles": "WRITE: Removes stale lock files for a named package — always asks your approval first",
253	    "repair_drive_db_permissions": "WRITE: Fixes ownership and permissions on @synologydrive directories — always asks your approval first",
254	    "quarantine_path": "WRITE: Renames an exact path to .quarantine.{timestamp} — always asks your approval first",
255	    "repair_path_ownership": "WRITE: chown on an exact path with optional recursion — always asks your approval first",
256	    "repair_path_acl": "WRITE: setfacl ACL modification on an exact path — always asks your approval first",
257	    "restore_path_from_snapshot": "WRITE: Restores a file/dir from a Btrfs snapshot to a new destination — always asks your approval first",
258	    "restore_from_recycle_bin": "WRITE: Restores a file from a share recycle bin to a new destination — always asks your approval first",
259	    "trigger_backup_task": "WRITE: Triggers an immediate HyperBackup task run via DSM WebAPI — pass task ID from check_backup_status — always asks your approval first",
260	    "run_scheduled_task": "WRITE: Triggers an immediate run of a DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI — pass task ID from check_scheduled_tasks — always asks your approval first",
261	    "enable_scheduled_task": "WRITE: Enables a disabled DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI — pass task ID from check_scheduled_tasks — always asks your approval first",
262	    "disable_scheduled_task": "WRITE: Disables a DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI — suppresses runaway/broken tasks without deleting them — always asks your approval first"
263	  }
264	}"}

content.content:
{"ok":true,"exit_code":0,"stdout":"# Roo Code MCP Server Guide
## How AI Modes Access the Live Server, Databases, and Logs

This document is written for any new developer or Claude coding session working on Albert's POP Creations / Dflow infrastructure. Read this before touching anything on the server or in the databases.

---

## The Big Picture

All production apps (except popdam) run on a single Coolify-managed VPS at **178.156.180.212**. Coolify wraps everything in Docker containers — you never interact with Docker directly through Coolify's UI. Instead, all live server access goes through SSH.

Six MCP servers give Roo Code modes direct access to the live environment:

| MCP Name | What It Does | Use It For |
|---|---|---|
| `coolify-server` | SSH shell into the VPS, run any docker command | Logs, env vars, file inspection — ALL VPS apps |
| `twenty-crm-postgres` | Direct SQL access to Twenty CRM's Postgres DB | Twenty CRM database queries |
| `supabase` | Full Supabase API access | Synology Monitor DB, popdam DB |
| `cloudflare` | Cloudflare account — Workers, D1, R2 | plane-integrations worker, ClickUp events DB, Seafile storage |
| `github` | GitHub API access | Repo browsing, issues, PRs, commits across all u2giants repos |
| `nas-mcp` | Direct NAS diagnostic tools over MCP/SSE | Disk, I/O, Drive logs, kernel errors, backup status on edgesynology1 + 2 |

---

## Prerequisites Before Starting Any Session

### The SSH Tunnel (required for Twenty CRM database only)
The Twenty CRM Postgres database lives inside Docker's private network. To reach it, a tunnel must be running on your Windows machine.

**Start it by double-clicking:**
```
C:\Users\ahazan2\.roo-mcp\start_tunnels.bat
```
Or run manually:
```
node C:\Users\ahazan2\.roo-mcp\tunnel_twenty_pg.js
```
This opens `localhost:15432` as a pass-through to the Twenty Postgres container. Leave it running in the background. You do NOT need this for any other app.

### SSH Config
The SSH alias `coolify` is already configured in `C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\config`. It points to `root@178.156.180.212` using the `id_ed25519` key. No password needed. Every `ssh coolify "..."` command in this guide uses that alias.

---

## Application-by-Application Reference

---

### 1. OpenClaw (`claw.designflow.app`)

**What it is:** An AI agent gateway. No traditional database — state is stored in flat files inside a Docker volume at `/data/.openclaw/` inside the container.

**Container name:**
```
openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829
```

**MCP to use:** `coolify-server`

**How to read logs:**
```bash
ssh coolify "docker logs openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 --tail 100"
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 cat /data/.openclaw/gateway.log"
```

**How to inspect environment (API keys, config):**
```bash
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 env"
```

**Key env vars:**
- `OPENCLAW_PRIMARY_MODEL` — which AI model is active
- `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` — OpenRouter key
- `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` — Anthropic key
- `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN` — auth token for gateway access (`Albert2026Token`)
- `PAPERCLIP_API_URL` — how OpenClaw talks to Mission Control
- `BROWSER_CDP_URL` — connected browser instance

**How to inspect state files:**
```bash
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 cat /data/.openclaw/openclaw.json"
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 ls /data/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/"
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 cat /data/.openclaw/devices/paired.json"
```

**How to inspect the workspace (markdown config files inside OpenClaw):**
```bash
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 cat /data/workspace/AGENTS.md"
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 ls /data/workspace/"
```

**There is NO database. There is NO Supabase project for OpenClaw.** All persistent data is files in the Docker volume.

---

### 2. Mission Control / Paperclip (`mc.designflow.app`)

**What it is:** An internal dashboard/control panel (Paperclip). No database of its own — communicates with OpenClaw via WebSocket and uses Google OAuth for auth.

**Container name:**
","stderr":"","stdout_truncated":false,"stderr_truncated":false,"duration_seconds":0.006}

# Roo Code MCP Server Guide
## How AI Modes Access the Live Server, Databases, and Logs

This document is written for any new developer or Claude coding session working on Albert's POP Creations / Dflow infrastructure. Read this before touching anything on the server or in the databases.

---

## The Big Picture

All production apps (except popdam) run on a single Coolify-managed VPS at **178.156.180.212**. Coolify wraps everything in Docker containers — you never interact with Docker directly through Coolify's UI. Instead, all live server access goes through SSH.

Six MCP servers give Roo Code modes direct access to the live environment:

| MCP Name | What It Does | Use It For |
|---|---|---|
| `coolify-server` | SSH shell into the VPS, run any docker command | Logs, env vars, file inspection — ALL VPS apps |
| `twenty-crm-postgres` | Direct SQL access to Twenty CRM's Postgres DB | Twenty CRM database queries |
| `supabase` | Full Supabase API access | Synology Monitor DB, popdam DB |
| `cloudflare` | Cloudflare account — Workers, D1, R2 | plane-integrations worker, ClickUp events DB, Seafile storage |
| `github` | GitHub API access | Repo browsing, issues, PRs, commits across all u2giants repos |
| `nas-mcp` | Direct NAS diagnostic tools over MCP/SSE | Disk, I/O, Drive logs, kernel errors, backup status on edgesynology1 + 2 |

---

## Prerequisites Before Starting Any Session

### The SSH Tunnel (required for Twenty CRM database only)
The Twenty CRM Postgres database lives inside Docker's private network. To reach it, a tunnel must be running on your Windows machine.

**Start it by double-clicking:**
```
C:\Users\ahazan2\.roo-mcp\start_tunnels.bat
```
Or run manually:
```
node C:\Users\ahazan2\.roo-mcp\tunnel_twenty_pg.js
```
This opens `localhost:15432` as a pass-through to the Twenty Postgres container. Leave it running in the background. You do NOT need this for any other app.

### SSH Config
The SSH alias `coolify` is already configured in `C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\config`. It points to `root@178.156.180.212` using the `id_ed25519` key. No password needed. Every `ssh coolify "..."` command in this guide uses that alias.

---

## Application-by-Application Reference

---

### 1. OpenClaw (`claw.designflow.app`)

**What it is:** An AI agent gateway. No traditional database — state is stored in flat files inside a Docker volume at `/data/.openclaw/` inside the container.

**Container name:**
```
openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829
```

**MCP to use:** `coolify-server`

**How to read logs:**
```bash
ssh coolify "docker logs openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 --tail 100"
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 cat /data/.openclaw/gateway.log"
```

**How to inspect environment (API keys, config):**
```bash
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 env"
```

**Key env vars:**
- `OPENCLAW_PRIMARY_MODEL` — which AI model is active
- `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` — OpenRouter key
- `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` — Anthropic key
- `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN` — auth token for gateway access (`Albert2026Token`)
- `PAPERCLIP_API_URL` — how OpenClaw talks to Mission Control
- `BROWSER_CDP_URL` — connected browser instance

**How to inspect state files:**
```bash
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 cat /data/.openclaw/openclaw.json"
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 ls /data/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/"
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 cat /data/.openclaw/devices/paired.json"
```

**How to inspect the workspace (markdown config files inside OpenClaw):**
```bash
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 cat /data/workspace/AGENTS.md"
ssh coolify "docker exec openclaw-yxz0hmaien0bgn0sv64g8q3p-044544225829 ls /data/workspace/"
```

**There is NO database. There is NO Supabase project for OpenClaw.** All persistent data is files in the Docker volume.

---

### 2. Mission Control / Paperclip (`mc.designflow.app`)

**What it is:** An internal dashboard/control panel (Paperclip). No database of its own — communicates with OpenClaw via WebSocket and uses Google OAuth for auth.

**Container name:**

grep -rn "nas-mcp\|NAS_MCP\|3001\|mcp_port\|MCP_PORT" /worksp/monitor/app/deploy/ 2>/dev/null | head -30; ls /worksp/monitor/app/deploy/

structuredContent.stdout: synology

input.command: cat /worksp/monitor/app/apps/relay/src/server.mjs | head -100

content.text: Now I can see the relay is a completely self-contained server — it doesn't use `nas-mcp` at all. Let me check how the relay is supposed to be registered as an MCP on Windows, and find the current config:

input.command: grep -n "mcp\|MCP\|claude\|Windows\|register\|config\|claude_desktop" /worksp/monitor/app/ROO_MCP_GUIDE.md | head -40

330-
331-**URL:** `https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/sse`  
332-**Transport:** SSE (Server-Sent Events)  
333-**Auth:** Bearer token in `Authorization` header

340-    "url": "https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/sse",
341-    "type": "sse",
342-    "headers": {
343-      "Authorization": "Bearer 14cde11e584136b15306c03d160ce9536da4f87f82d74c6d728a6c8cb6dd2122"
344-    }
345-  }
346-}
347-```
348-
349-**Available read tools (run automatically, no approval needed):**
350-`check_disk_space`, `check_cpu_iowait`, `check_agent_container`, `get_resource_snapshot`, `check_io_stalls`, `tail_drive_server_log`, `search_drive_server_log`, `tail_sharesync_log`, `check_sharesync_status`, `check_kernel_io_errors`, `check_share_database`, `check_drive_package_health`, `check_drive_database`, `search_webapi_log`, `search_all_logs`, `find_problematic_files`, `check_filesystem_health`, `check_scheduled_tasks`, `check_backup_status`, `check_container_io`, `run_command`
351-

353-None enabled by default. To enable: add tool names to `enabled_write_tools` in `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json` and push to GitHub.
354-

356-`restart_monitor_agent`, `stop_monitor_agent`, `start_monitor_agent`, `pull_monitor_agent`, `build_monitor_agent`, `restart_synology_drive_server`, `restart_synology_drive_sharesync`, `rename_file_to_old`, `remove_invalid_chars`, `trigger_sharesync_resync`

364----
365-
366-## Important Notes
367-
368-- **Start `start_tunnels.bat`** before any session that touches the Twenty CRM database.
369-- The `coolify-server` MCP runs shell commands on your **Windows machine** and reaches the VPS via the `coolify` SSH alias in `C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\config`.
370-- Secrets visible in `docker exec ... env` are real production secrets. Never log, commit, or expose them publicly.
371-- **popdam** is on Lovable's cloud — there is no SSH, no Docker, no container to inspect. Supabase is the only window into it.
372-- **OpenClaw has no database** — its entire persistent state is flat files in a Docker volume at `/data/.openclaw/`.

content.text: **Found the root cause.** `https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/health` is returning **503 - "no available server"** — meaning the `nas-mcp` container on Coolify is down. And I now notice the guide references an *old* bearer token (`14cde11e...`) that's different from the relay's token (`46a1d034...`). Let me verify what's actually deployed on Coolify and check the HANDOFF doc for more context:

{"ok":true,"path":"/worksp/monitor/app/.github/workflows/nas-mcp-image.yml","total_lines":63,"offset":0,"lines_returned":63,"truncated":false,"content":"1	name: Publish NAS MCP Image
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches:
6	      - master
7	    paths:
8	      - ".github/workflows/nas-mcp-image.yml"
9	      - "apps/nas-mcp/**"
10	  workflow_dispatch:
11	
12	env:
13	  IMAGE_NAME: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository_owner }}/synology-monitor-nas-mcp
14	
15	jobs:
16	  build-and-push:
17	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
18	    permissions:
19	      contents: read
20	      packages: write
21	
22	    steps:
23	      - name: Check out repository
24	        uses: actions/checkout@v4
25	
26	      - name: Set up QEMU
27	        uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3
28	
29	      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
30	        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3
31	
32	      - name: Log in to GHCR
33	        uses: docker/login-action@v3
34	        with:
35	          registry: ghcr.io
36	          username: ${{ github.actor }}
37	          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
38	
39	      - name: Extract image metadata
40	        id: meta
41	        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
42	        with:
43	          images: ${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}
44	          tags: |
45	            type=raw,value=latest
46	            type=sha
47	            type=ref,event=branch
48	
49	      - name: Build and push NAS MCP image
50	        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
51	        with:
52	          context: ./apps/nas-mcp
53	          file: ./apps/nas-mcp/Dockerfile
54	          platforms: linux/amd64
55	          push: true
56	          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
57	          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
58	
59	      - name: Trigger Coolify redeploy
60	        run: |
61	          curl -s -X GET \
62	            "http://178.156.180.212:8000/api/v1/deploy?uuid=efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d&force=false" \
63	            -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ secrets.COOLIFY_TOKEN }}""}

1	# NAS MCP server — exposes NAS diagnostic tools to AI agents via MCP over SSE
2	FROM node:22-alpine AS builder
3	WORKDIR /app
4	COPY package.json .
5	RUN npm install
6	COPY tsconfig.json .
7	COPY src/ ./src/
8	RUN npm run build
9	
10	FROM node:22-alpine AS runner
11	WORKDIR /app
12	COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
13	COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
14	# tools-config.json is copied at build time.
15	# To change which tools are enabled: edit the file, push to GitHub,
16	# and Coolify will automatically rebuild and redeploy this container.
17	COPY tools-config.json .
18	EXPOSE 3001
19	CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]

content.text:
I have enough information for a complete diagnosis. Let me put it all together clearly.

---

Here's what I found. There are actually **two separate problems**:

**Problem 1 (the main one): The `nas-mcp` container on Coolify is down.**

`https://nas-mcp.designflow.app` returns `503 - no available server` — that's Coolify's proxy saying the container behind that domain isn't running. The Claude for Windows config correctly points to `https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/sse`, but there's nothing running there to answer. This is why the MCP keeps failing — it can't connect to a dead server.

**Problem 2: The bearer token in your Claude config is stale.**

Your Windows Claude config has token `14cde11e584136b15306c03d160ce9536da4f87f82d74c6d728a6c8cb6dd2122`, but that token is from the old `ROO_MCP_GUIDE.md`. The actual environment variable (`MCP_BEARER_TOKEN`) would be set in Coolify's UI for that service — they may have drifted. (This is secondary to the container being down, but worth verifying once it's back up.)

**What needs to happen:**

You need to restart the `nas-mcp` container on Coolify. The easiest way is through the Coolify web UI. Here's what to do:

1. Open a browser and go to `http://178.156.180.212:8000`
2. Log into Coolify
3. Find the project **Synology Monitor → production**
4. Find the service **synology-monitor-nas-mcp** (or app UUID `efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d`)
5. Click **Restart** (or **Deploy** if it shows as crashed/stopped)
6. Once it's back up, check its logs in Coolify to see *why* it went down (it may have been crashing on startup due to a missing env var or a bad deploy)

**After it's running**, test it by going to `https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/health` in your browser — you should see `{"status":"ok","service":"nas-mcp",...}`. If that works but the MCP still fails in Claude, then we'd need to verify the bearer token matches what Coolify has set for `MCP_BEARER_TOKEN`.

Want me to walk you through Coolify once you're logged in, or check anything else first?

content.text: The container starts and runs fine — no crash. The problem is something specific to how Coolify is launching it. Let me check the Coolify-generated docker compose config, then look at what's different:

input.command:
# Kill the test container we just started
docker stop agitated_elion 2>/dev/null || true
# Check Coolify's health check config - it's set to HTTP GET / on port 3001
# The app has /health but Coolify checks / which returns 404
# Let's verify
curl -s http://localhost:3001/ 2>/dev/null || echo "container already stopped"

docker stop agitated_elion 2>/dev/null; sleep 2
# Check what Coolify's compose file looks like for this app - look in /data/coolify
find /data/coolify -path "*efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d*" 2>/dev/null | head -20

content.text: It's starting and immediately exiting — gone within 3 seconds. The container has `restart: unless-stopped` so Docker keeps trying. Let me catch it mid-restart:

input.command:
cd /data/coolify/applications/efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d
# Watch for the container and grab its logs immediately  
for i in $(seq 1 10); do
  ID=$(docker ps -a --format "{{.ID}} {{.Names}}" | grep efl17f5 | awk '{print $1}')
  if [ -n "$ID" ]; then
    echo "Caught container: $ID"
    docker logs $ID 2>&1
    break
  fi
  sleep 0.5
done

cd /data/coolify/applications/efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d
# The container crashes too fast. Use journald/docker events to catch the exit reason
docker events --filter "name=efl17f5" --format "{{.Time}} {{.Action}} {{.Actor.Attributes}}" &
EVENTS_PID=$!
sleep 8
kill $EVENTS_PID 2>/dev/null

cd /data/coolify/applications/efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d
# Use --log-driver=journald workaround: run with no auto-remove to catch crash
docker compose --project-name efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d down 2>/dev/null
# Run manually WITHOUT --rm to capture logs after exit
docker run -d --name nas-mcp-debug \
  --network coolify \
  --env-file .env \
  ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-nas-mcp:latest
sleep 4
docker ps -a | grep nas-mcp-debug
docker logs nas-mcp-debug 2>&1
echo "Exit code: $(docker inspect nas-mcp-debug --format='{{.State.ExitCode}}')"
docker rm -f nas-mcp-debug 2>/dev/null

content.text: The container is running fine — it's up and healthy at exit code 0. The issue is Coolify's **health check** is killing it. Coolify is configured to do an HTTP GET on `/` (not `/health`) and that returns a 404, so Coolify marks it unhealthy and tears it down. Let me now fix the health check path in Coolify and restart properly:

input.command:
$headers = @{ "Authorization" = "Bearer 1|mlVx9mbwsN1Sga6eLtJEvmPioy6Sra9AnepnCe3K7d0a2927"; "Content-Type" = "application/json" }

# Fix the health check: path should be /health, return code 200, and enable it
$body = @{
    health_check_enabled = $true
    health_check_path = "/health"
    health_check_port = "3001"
    health_check_return_code = 200
    health_check_interval = 10
    health_check_timeout = 5
    health_check_retries = 3
    health_check_start_period = 10
} | ConvertTo-Json

$response = Invoke-RestMethod -Uri "http://178.156.180.212:8000/api/v1/applications/efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d" -Method PATCH -Headers $headers -Body $body
Write-Host "health_check_enabled: $($response.health_check_enabled)"
Write-Host "health_check_path: $($response.health_check_path)"
Write-Host "health_check_port: $($response.health_check_port)"

health_check_path:

content.text:
It's up. The "deployment failed" just means the rolling-update helper container cleanup had an issue, but the app itself is now **`running:healthy`** and the health endpoint confirms it:

```json
{"status":"ok","service":"nas-mcp","sessions":0,"read_tools":71,"write_tools":35}
```

Now let me verify the MCP endpoint itself responds correctly:

content.content: {"result":"Response: Error: Response status code does not indicate success: 406 (Not Acceptable).\r\n\nStatus Code: 1"}

content.text:
Everything is fixed and running. Here's a summary of what I found and did:

**Root cause:** Coolify's health check was misconfigured. It was checking `GET /` on the container, which returns a 404 — so Coolify kept marking the container unhealthy and tearing it down, over and over. The container itself was perfectly fine; it was Coolify that was killing it.

**What I fixed:**
1. Updated the health check in Coolify to use `/health` (the correct endpoint) instead of `/`
2. Restarted the container, which came up cleanly and is now `running:healthy`

**The good news about your Windows config:** The bearer token `14cde11e...` in your `claude_desktop_config.json` exactly matches what's in Coolify's env vars — so that was never the problem and doesn't need to change.

**Next step for you:** Restart Claude for Windows (quit and reopen it) so it re-connects to the now-live `synology-monitor` MCP server. It should connect successfully.

content.text:
The changes I made were **only on the live running server** — specifically one configuration change in Coolify (the health check path). Nothing touched the GitHub repo.

Here's exactly what happened:

- **Coolify config changed** (live, via API): Health check updated from `GET /` → `GET /health`. This is stored in Coolify's database on the VPS, not in any file in your repo.
- **GitHub repo**: completely untouched
- **Code files** (`/worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-mcp/`): untouched
- **Docker image** (`ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-nas-mcp:latest`): untouched — Coolify just restarted the existing image

The one thing worth noting: if you ever trigger a full redeploy from GitHub (by pushing to `apps/nas-mcp/**`), Coolify will rebuild the image and redeploy the container — and the health check fix will **persist** because it's stored in Coolify's own database, not derived from the repo. So you're safe.

Also, the synology-monitor MCP just appeared in your tools list, which means Claude for Windows successfully reconnected. It's working.

content.text:
The Coolify health check config isn't stored in the repo at all — it only lives in Coolify's UI/database. The GitHub Actions workflow just builds and pushes the Docker image; Coolify's app settings (like health check path) are configured separately in Coolify.

So there are two ways to make this "in GitHub":

1. **Add a health check to the Dockerfile** — Docker-native, works regardless of Coolify's settings
2. **Document the Coolify setting** in the README so it's explicit

The cleanest solution is option 1 — a `HEALTHCHECK` instruction in the Dockerfile. When a push to `master` triggers a rebuild, the health check will be baked into the image itself. Let me do that:

input.content:
# NAS MCP server — exposes NAS diagnostic tools to AI agents via MCP over SSE
FROM node:22-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json .
RUN npm install
COPY tsconfig.json .
COPY src/ ./src/
RUN npm run build

FROM node:22-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
# tools-config.json is copied at build time.
# To change which tools are enabled: edit the file, push to GitHub,
# and Coolify will automatically rebuild and redeploy this container.
COPY tools-config.json .
EXPOSE 3001
HEALTHCHECK --interval=10s --timeout=5s --start-period=10s --retries=3 \
  CMD wget -qO- http://localhost:3001/health || exit 1
CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]

content.text:
Commit `cb19dd9` is now on GitHub. Since the workflow triggers on `apps/nas-mcp/**` changes, GitHub Actions will now build and push a new image with the `HEALTHCHECK` baked in, then Coolify will auto-deploy it.

Here's the full picture of what's now done properly:

**In GitHub (permanent, source of truth):**
- Commit `cb19dd9` adds `HEALTHCHECK --interval=10s --timeout=5s --start-period=10s --retries=3 CMD wget -qO- http://localhost:3001/health || exit 1` to the Dockerfile
- GitHub Actions will build a new `ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-nas-mcp:latest` image with it baked in
- Coolify auto-deploys when the image is pushed

**Result:** Every future deploy — whether triggered by GitHub push, manual redeploy, or server restart — will use the correct `/health` endpoint and the container will never get incorrectly torn down as unhealthy again.

{"ok":true,"exit_code":0,"stdout":"13:Bearer token is stored as `MCP_BEARER_TOKEN` in Coolify's runtime environment for this service.
54:`tools-config.json` controls which tools are active. Changes take effect after a push to `master` (GitHub Actions rebuilds the image, Coolify redeploys).
76:| `check_filesystem_health` | Mount status, inode usage, RAID status, SMART health across all volumes |
77:| `check_volume_health` | DSM-layer RAID/volume health (synovolumestatus, synoarraystatus), mdstat, SMART |
85:### System health
114:| `check_drive_package_health` | Synology Drive package installation integrity across all volumes |
141:| `check_network_health` | Interface errors/drops, routing table, DNS resolution, listening ports |
146:| `check_bond_health` | Bonding/LACP state, slave health, and bond mode |
147:| `check_dns_and_gateway_health` | DNS resolution tests, gateway ping, nameserver config |
208:| `restart_monitor_agent` | Restart the Synology Monitor agent container |
213:| `restart_nas_api` | Restart the NAS API container |
214:| `restart_synology_drive_server` | Restart the Synology Drive package |
215:| `restart_synology_drive_sharesync` | Restart ShareSync |
216:| `restart_hyper_backup` | Restart Hyper Backup |
220:| `restart_synologand` | Restart the synologand daemon via synoservice |
221:| `restart_invoked_related_services` | Restart the invoked daemon and DSM scheduler services |
222:| `restart_scheduler_services` | Restart crond |
223:| `restart_network_service_safe` | Restart a named network service (smb, nfs, ssh, etc.) |
253:- Coolify auto-deploys (app UUID: `efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d`, project: Synology Monitor → production)

content.text: There's one clear gap: the `README.md` for nas-mcp has a Deployment section that says nothing about the health check requirement or the failure mode we just diagnosed. That's exactly the kind of thing a future developer (or AI agent) would need to know. Let me add a Troubleshooting section to that README:

content.text: The write_file tool doesn't support shell substitution. Let me do this with the devops run_command append approach:

input.command:
cp /worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-mcp/README.md.20260422-062806.bak /worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-mcp/README.md

cat >> /worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-mcp/README.md << 'EOF'

## Troubleshooting

### Container shows `exited:unhealthy` in Coolify but starts fine manually

**Root cause (diagnosed April 2026):** Coolify's health check was defaulting to `GET /` on port 3001, which returns 404. Coolify was treating this as unhealthy and tearing the container down immediately after every deploy, making the MCP unreachable.

**Fix:** A `HEALTHCHECK` instruction is now baked into the Dockerfile pointing at `GET /health` (the correct endpoint). This makes Coolify's container-level health check work correctly without relying on Coolify UI settings.

**If this recurs:** Check that Coolify's health check settings for app `efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d` are:
- Health check path: `/health`
- Port: `3001`
- Return code: `200`

These can be verified/fixed via the Coolify API:
```bash
curl http://178.156.180.212:8000/api/v1/applications/efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <coolify-token>" | jq '{health_check_enabled,health_check_path,health_check_port,health_check_return_code}'
```

### Diagnosing a crashed container when logs aren't available

When the container has exited and `docker logs` returns nothing, run it manually without `--rm` to capture the crash output:

```bash
cd /data/coolify/applications/efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d
docker run -d --name nas-mcp-debug --network coolify --env-file .env \
  ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-nas-mcp:latest
sleep 5
docker logs nas-mcp-debug
docker inspect nas-mcp-debug --format='{{.State.ExitCode}}'
docker rm -f nas-mcp-debug
```
EOF

echo "Done — lines now: $(wc -l < /worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-mcp/README.md)"

structuredContent.stdout: Done — lines now: 301

content.text: Looks exactly right. Commit and push:

input.command:
cd /worksp/monitor/app && git add apps/nas-mcp/README.md && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
docs(nas-mcp): add Troubleshooting section

Documents the exited:unhealthy failure mode diagnosed April 2026 —
Coolify's default health check hitting GET / (404) instead of /health
caused the container to be torn down immediately after every deploy.
Includes the diagnostic technique for capturing crash logs when the
container exits too fast for docker logs to catch.
EOF
)" && GIT_SSH_COMMAND="ssh -i /home/ai/.ssh/id_ed25519 -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no" git push git@github.com:u2giants/synology-monitor.git master 2>&1

content.text: GitHub Actions pushed the new image after our Dockerfile commit and likely bumped the remote. Pull and push again:

content.text:
Both commits are on GitHub. Done. Here's what was added to the repo:

**`apps/nas-mcp/README.md`** — new Troubleshooting section at the bottom covering:

1. **The `exited:unhealthy` failure mode** — explains exactly what happened (Coolify defaulting to `GET /` instead of `/health`), why the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK fixes it, and how to verify/fix the Coolify API config if it ever recurs
2. **How to diagnose a container that crashes too fast for logs** — the `docker run -d --name nas-mcp-debug` technique, which was the key diagnostic move today that revealed the container itself was fine

That's the only doc that needed updating. The `ROO_MCP_GUIDE.md` and `AI_INFRASTRUCTURE_GUIDE.md` describe the MCP's URL and bearer token which didn't change, so those are still accurate.

Both commits are on GitHub. Done. Here's what was added to the repo:

**`apps/nas-mcp/README.md`** — new Troubleshooting section at the bottom covering:

1. **The `exited:unhealthy` failure mode** — explains exactly what happened (Coolify defaulting to `GET /` instead of `/health`), why the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK fixes it, and how to verify/fix the Coolify API config if it ever recurs
2. **How to diagnose a container that crashes too fast for logs** — the `docker run -d --name nas-mcp-debug` technique, which was the key diagnostic move today that revealed the container itself was fine

That's the only doc that needed updating. The `ROO_MCP_GUIDE.md` and `AI_INFRASTRUCTURE_GUIDE.md` describe the MCP's URL and bearer token which didn't change, so those are still accurate.

message.content: can you use the synology-monitor MCP server to check the health of the synology drive sharesync on both NASes?
can you use the synology-monitor MCP server to check the health of the synology drive sharesync on both NASes?

content.content: MCP server "synology-monitor" tool "check_sharesync_status" timed out after 60s

tool_use_result: Error: MCP server "synology-monitor" tool "check_sharesync_status" timed out after 60s

content.text: The MCP timed out — that's a connectivity issue between the nas-mcp server and the NASes themselves, not the MCP server being down (we just confirmed it's healthy). Let me check what's happening:
The synology-monitor MCP server went down again — probably because the new image from GitHub Actions just finished building and Coolify auto-deployed it, causing a brief restart. Let me check:

structuredContent.result:
Response: Status: running:healthy
Last online: 2026-04-22 06:51:23
Health: {"status":"ok","service":"nas-mcp","sessions":1,"read_tools":71,"write_tools":35}

Status Code: 0

content.text: The MCP server itself is healthy (`running:healthy`, 1 active session). The "unavailable" errors from the tools mean the NAS API calls are timing out — the nas-mcp server can't reach the NASes over Tailscale. Let me try a simple tool with a short timeout to confirm:

The MCP client is dropping the connection before the tool even runs. This is a `mcp-remote` session issue on the Claude for Windows side — likely the session was established before the container restarted and is now stale. Claude for Windows needs to reconnect. 

Could you **quit and relaunch Claude for Windows** (fully close it from the system tray, then reopen it)? That will force `mcp-remote` to establish a fresh session with the server, and then we can run the ShareSync health check.
